title: Chapter 27: The Choice
content: I'm falling.

Not the way you fall in dreams — no stomach drop, no wind, no ground rushing up. This is the other kind. The kind where there's nothing to fall through, nothing to fall toward, just the endless white void stretching in every direction and me suspended inside it like a speck of dust in a room where someone turned off the light and forgot to turn it back on.

The cold hits first. Not winter cold, not the kind you can fix with a coat. This is absence cold. The temperature of nothing. It seeps through my skin and into my chest and settles there like a second heartbeat made of ice.
Then the timelines appear.

They ripple around me — three of them — projected onto the fog of the void like film on smoke, half-transparent and shimmering. I can smell them before I can see them clearly. Wall Street: coffee and dry-cleaning fluid and the metallic tang of ambition. Paris: perfume and wine and the particular sweetness of cigarette smoke on rain. Clonakilty: salt and bread and the wool-warm smell of a man who works with his hands.
And then I hear them. Fragments. Every moment that mattered, tumbling through the white like confetti made of memory.
Julian A, standing in his office with his sleeves rolled up and his tie undone, saying: "You don't have to carry it alone."
Julian B, lying on a gallery floor at midnight, paint on his jaw, saying: "Reste."
Julian C, in his kitchen with flour on his hands and his voice cracking at the edges, saying: "I don't want to do this alone anymore."
Three versions of the same man. Three versions of myself. Three entire lives, spinning around me like planets I've been orbiting for months, unable to land.

And underneath it all, the Composite's words — his words, all of him, distilled into one impossible voice: *Choose the world where you're most yourself. Because that's where I'll be most myself, too.*
The void hums. The timelines shimmer. And I stop falling.
I'm just — here. Suspended. Floating in the nothing between everythings with three lives displayed around me like exhibits in a museum I can never leave without choosing.

So I look. Really look. Not the way I've been looking for weeks — darting between timelines, grabbing at happiness like a child snatching sweets, always with one foot out the door. Not through the lens of escape. Through the lens of truth.

Timeline A. Wall Street. I see myself in that glass-walled conference room, thirty-second floor, the city a glittering bruise below me. I'm competent. I'm growing. I've learned to hold my ground in rooms full of men who think I'm decorative until I open my mouth and prove them wrong. I've built something real there — a career, a footing, a spine that didn't used to exist. Julian A taught me how to stand. His faith in me was a scaffold I could climb.
But.

I went to Wall Street because I was afraid. Afraid of not being successful enough. Afraid of being the girl who couldn't hack it. Afraid of becoming my mother's prophecy — the one who tried and failed and settled. Fear put me on that train to Manhattan. Not desire. Not love. Not the aching pull toward something I couldn't live without. Just fear, dressed up in ambition's clothing, smiling through its teeth.
The truth tastes like pennies on my tongue. Metallic. Wrong.

Timeline B. Paris. I see myself in that apartment with the iron balcony and the light that pours in like honey, and I'm beautiful. I'm admired. I'm wrapped in someone who sees me — really sees me — and paints me like I'm worth looking at forever. Julian B's love is a gallery. I'm the centrepiece. He orbits me like I'm the sun, and for a while, that felt like the most intoxicating thing in the world. To be seen. To be wanted. To be someone's masterpiece.
But.

I went to Paris because I was afraid of not being special enough. Afraid of being ordinary. Afraid that without the art and the city and the beautiful man, I'd just be another forgettable face in an unremarkable life. Ego put me on that plane. Not truth. Not the bone-deep knowledge that this was where I belonged. Just the hungry, hollow need to matter.
The truth smells like turpentine and regret.

Timeline C. Clonakilty. I see myself in that café with the crooked counter and the window that fogs when it rains. I'm quiet. I'm making lattes. I'm feeding a stray cat that showed up one morning and never left. I'm loving a man who would give up his business, his home, his entire carefully constructed life for a stranger — and nearly did, for me. Julian C doesn't orbit me. He stands beside me. When I fall, he doesn't catch me — he falls too, and we figure out how to get up together.

And the truth is: I went to Ireland because I was afraid of everything. Afraid of failing. Afraid of succeeding. Afraid of choosing wrong. I ran to the furthest place I could find because I thought if I was far enough away from everything, the fear would lose its GPS signal and stop finding me.
It didn't. The fear came with me. It always does.

But here's the thing about Clonakilty. About Julian C. About that drafty flat above the chemist and the café that doesn't turn a profit and the life that nobody would ever make a movie about — in that place, surrounded by those people, the fear stopped mattering. Not because it went away. Because there were things more important than being afraid.
Him. Them. The life we were building between crises. The ordinary, unremarkable, desperately important life.
The truth feels like wool against my skin. Warm. Scratchy. Real.
I close my eyes in the void, and I think about three AM.

In New York, at three AM, I'm sitting at my desk staring at spreadsheets, and the numbers blur, and I hate myself. Not dramatically. Quietly. The way you hate something you can't stop doing. I'm successful and I'm impressive and I'm drowning in a life I built to prove a point no one was arguing.

In Paris, at three AM, I'm lying in Julian B's bed, and he's asleep, and I'm arranging my face into the expression he likes best, and I realize I'm posing for no one. Performing for an empty room. I'm beautiful and I'm adored and I'm playing a character in a love story about myself.
In Clonakilty, at three AM—

I'm on the boat. In the dark. Julian C's arm around my shoulders, heavy and warm and utterly unselfconscious. He's not holding me like a painting. He's holding me like an anchor. Like I'm the thing keeping him from drifting. And I'm not thinking about myself at all.

I'm thinking about him. About the way he laughs when he's surprised. About the stubble on his jaw that he always misses when he shaves. About how he puts too much milk in the tea and hums off-key when he thinks no one's listening. About the life we're building between his disasters and mine, brick by unglamorous brick.
At three AM in Clonakilty, I'm not afraid. Not because there's nothing to be afraid of. Because someone needs me more than I need my own fear.
That's the self I want to be.

Not the successful one — she's just fear in a power suit. Not the beautiful one — she's just ego in designer clothes. The one who forgets to be afraid. The one who looks outward instead of in. The one who chose a life so small and so ordinary that the only way to survive it is to stop performing and just — be.
That's who I am. That's who I want to be.
I open my eyes.

The timelines are shaking. The void is contracting. The white is getting whiter, pressing in, and I can feel the Garden eating itself — the forking paths collapsing, the lattices buckling, the entire architecture of choice coming apart like a house in a hurricane.
I have seconds. Maybe less.

I reach into my pocket, and my phone is there, even in the void, even in the impossible white. Of course it is. The Fork app has always been with me, through every shift, every leap, every life. It's the only constant in a reality that won't stay still.
I call Mika.
The phone rings once, twice — and then her voice, crackling through static, but unmistakably her: "Aves? What's happening? Where are you?"
"I'm in the void," I say, and I laugh, because that's insane, but it's also true. "The Garden's collapsing. I have to choose. Now."
Silence. Not the cold silence of the void. Mika-silence. The kind that means she's thinking, hard, and won't waste my time with anything less than essential.
"I'm choosing," I say, and my voice shakes. I can feel it in my throat, the tremor, the way the words want to buckle. "I'm choosing Ireland."
Another silence. Longer this time. I can hear the static like rain on a window.
Then: "Is that where you're happiest?"
And I say — because Mika asked me to think about what happens at zero, and I have, and the answer isn't simple, and she deserves the truth — "No."
More static. The void presses closer. The timelines are dimming at the edges, bleeding at the seams, and I can feel the warmth of each path flickering like a candle in a draft.
"It's where I'm most myself."
A long pause. The longest pause of my life, suspended in white nothing, holding a phone to my ear, watching three lives burn.
"Then that's the right choice, Aves."

Her voice breaks on my name. Or maybe that's me breaking. Maybe we're both breaking, a thousand miles and a million realities apart, connected by a signal that shouldn't exist in a void that shouldn't exist, choosing together even though only one of us can make the call.
A sob tears out of me. Raw. Animal. "I love you."
"I love you too." Mika's voice is steady now, the way it gets when she's crying and won't let you hear it. "Now go get your man."
The phone goes dead. The void surges.
I reach for the Clonakilty path.

My fingers brush it, and the warmth is immediate — not the void's absence-cold, not the sterile nothing of the in-between. This is real warmth. Like a sun-warmed stone bench in July. Like an arm around my shoulders on a boat in the dark. Like the hand of someone who's been waiting, patient and steady, for me to finally, finally come home.
I grab it. Both hands. I hold on like it's the edge of a cliff and the void is the drop.
And the other two paths begin to dim.

Timeline A first. Wall Street. Julian A's office fading like a photograph left in sunlight. The glass walls, the city view, the way he looked at me across the conference table like I'd invented the sun. I see his hands — the way they held mine like something precious, something worth protecting. I see his face. I see the life we built, competence and growing and standing my ground, and it was real, it was real, it was—
Gone. The light scatters. Julian A dissolves into white.

Timeline B. Paris. Julian B's gallery dissolving around him like rain washing watercolour from a wall. The paint-stained floor, the iron balcony, the way he said *reste* like it was the only word he knew. I see his eyes — dark, consuming, seeing me like I was the only true thing in a world of fakes. I see the life we made, beauty and admiration and being someone's masterpiece, and it was gorgeous, it was gorgeous, it was—
Gone. The colour runs. Julian B fades into nothing.
And I'm screaming.

I'm crying and screaming in the void, holding onto the Clonakilty path with both hands, watching two lives dissolve like morning fog. Because they were real. Those Julians were real. The love I felt in those timelines was real, not a simulation, not a shadow, not a cheap copy — real, full-blooded, aching, true love, and I'm killing it. I'm choosing to let it die. Every beautiful moment, every terrible fight, every extraordinary ordinary second in those timelines is turning to light and then to nothing, and the grief is physical — a fist in my chest, a blade in my throat, a sound coming out of me that I've never made before and never want to make again.
The best parts of two lives. Gone. Because I chose.
Because the alternative was losing all three.
The void condenses.

The white doesn't fade — it thickens, compresses, becomes something with weight and texture and smell. The Garden of Forking Paths is gone. The lattices, the doorways, the infinite corridors of maybe — all gone. There's no more forking. No more paths. Just one.
I'm standing on solid ground.

The cold of the void is gone, replaced by a chill that's different — real, specific, Irish. Sea wind cutting across my face, carrying salt and seaweed and the green smell of grass after rain. My feet are on cobblestones. Worn, uneven, familiar cobblestones that I know like I know my own heartbeat.
Clonakilty.

The café is behind me. I can feel its warmth on my back without turning around, the way you feel a fire when your back's to it. The sea is ahead, grey and restless, doing what it always does — moving, breathing, not caring whether I'm here or not.
And Julian C is in the doorway.

He's holding a coffee cup — the chipped one, the one with the green rim that he won't throw away because it was his mother's. He's looking at me like I'm a ghost. Not the startled look of someone who's been surprised. The stunned look of someone who's been waiting for something so long they'd stopped believing it would come.
"You're really here," he says.

Not *you came back*. Not *you shifted*. Something more fundamental. Something that acknowledges what this is: not a visit. Not a trial run. Not another hop between timelines. I'm here. The kind of here that means I'm not leaving.

I walk to him. My legs are shaking. The cobblestones are cold through my shoes. The sea wind pulls at my hair. Every step is a choice, and I keep choosing, and I keep choosing, and I keep choosing this.

I reach him. I take the coffee cup out of his hand — his fingers are warm, the ceramic is warm, everything is warm, I'm not cold anymore — and I set it down on the counter behind him. It makes a small sound against the wood. A real sound. The most real sound I've ever heard.
"I'm staying," I say. "Not shifting. Not visiting. Staying."

He opens his mouth. I can see the questions forming, the fears, the hope he doesn't trust yet, and I don't want him to say any of it. I don't want him to ask if I'm sure. I don't want him to give me an out. I just want him to be here, in this moment, with me, in the only life I have left.
I put my hand over his mouth. Gentle. His breath is warm against my palm.
"Don't say anything yet," I whisper. "Just — be here with me."

He nods against my palm. His eyes are bright. Not with tears — he doesn't cry, this Julian, he just goes quiet and still and present in a way that cracks me open wider than any tears ever could. His hand comes up and wraps around my wrist. Not pulling. Not pushing. Just holding on.
Behind us, I hear a sound. Soft. Electronic. The café counter.
I turn.
The Fork app is on my phone, which is on the counter where I left it hours ago or months ago or a lifetime ago. The screen glows.
INTEGRITY: 1%
I watch it. The number feels like a heartbeat. One percent. One life. One choice.
The screen goes dark.
The app icon flickers once. Twice.
And then — nothing. The icon vanishes. Not grayed out, not disabled, not waiting to be reopened. Gone. Like it was never there at all.
The Fork app is gone.

No more paths. No more choices. No more doors opening onto other lives, other Julians, other Averys who might have been happier or sadder or more successful or more beautiful or more afraid. Just this one. This life. This man. This café with its crooked counter and its foggy windows and its cat that won't leave.
My phone screen flashes once more. A final message, there and gone so fast I almost miss it:
INTEGRITY: STABLE.

The app didn't die. I realize that now, standing in the warmth of this café with Julian C's hand around my wrist and the sound of the sea in my ears. It fulfilled its purpose. It existed to help me choose. To show me the paths and let me walk them and learn who I was on each one until I could tell the difference between the life I wanted and the life I needed.
I chose.

I'm crying again. But it's different now. Not the screaming, shredding grief of the void. This is quieter. Aftershock tears. The kind that come after everything important has already happened and your body is just catching up.

Julian's hand tightens on my wrist. He's still not speaking. I asked him not to, and he's listening, and that's the most Julian C thing he could possibly do. Not grand gestures. Not declarations. Just presence. Just being here.
I lean into him. His arm comes around my shoulders — heavy, warm, unselfconscious. The way it was on the boat at three AM. The way it always is. Like an anchor. Like home.

The café smells like coffee and bread and the particular sweetness of rain on old wood. My shoes are wet. My face is a mess. The Fork app is gone. Two entire lives full of people I loved are nothing but light and memory and the fading echo of a version of me who doesn't exist anymore.
And for the first time in months, there's only one life to live.
I close my eyes.
I'm not afraid.
